turns.text: Hello Esperanza, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It is strange to put it into words, really. Sometimes, when the snow is falling outside and the house is so quiet it rings in my ears, I feel like I am just drifting. 

I’m thirty-five, and this is my third baby, so you’d think I would feel like an expert by now. But everything here feels… heavy. Back home, my mother and my sisters would be around me. They would know if I looked tired or if I wasn't eating right just by the way I carried my shoulders. Here, I just have my supplement bottles. I line them up on the counter every morning like a little army. I take the iron, the methylfolate, the DHA—I’m terrified that if I miss one, I’m failing him. 

The clinic? They are polite, yes. But they see me as a “medium risk” chart. When I try to tell them that the greens here don’t taste like the ones I grew up with, or that I’m worried because I’m so far from the hospital, they look at the clock. It’s like I’m a complication they have to manage within fifteen minutes. I feel like I’m shouting behind a glass wall, and they only see my lips moving. Does that make sense? It’s… lonely.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

To be honest, it is hard to say there is a "relationship" at all. It feels more like a transaction. I go into the clinic, I sit on that cold paper—that sound, the crinkle of it, it always makes me shiver—and I answer their questions. They check the blood pressure, they listen to the heartbeat for those few precious seconds, and then they mark something down. 

I want someone to look at me—not at the chart, not at the screen—and just ask me how I am carrying the fear of being so far from home. But the midwife, she is always so busy. Her hand is practically already on the door handle before I’ve even finished my sentence. I think she thinks she is being efficient, helping me by keeping things moving, but it just makes me feel like I am just another "medium risk" box to check. 

I find myself wishing for someone who speaks my language, not just the words, but the feeling of what it’s like to be an outsider. I don't need another pamphlet on nutrition. I need someone to acknowledge that I am trying my best to feed this baby with what I can find in these grocery stores. It is a very small, quiet kind of heartbreak, being ignored while they are trying so hard to be "professional."
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

That is the thing—there isn’t really anyone else. Out here in the woods of Wrentham, the community is beautiful, but it is not *my* community. My husband, he tries, but he is gone so much for his work. When he is away, it is just me and the silence. 

I’ve looked for support groups or someone to talk to, maybe another mother who understands what it is like to move across an ocean and then try to build a family in a place where the winters feel like they last forever. But between the language barrier and the distance, I haven't found a bridge. There are no interpreters offered at my clinic, even though my English is… well, it is enough to get by, but it is not enough to explain the deep, gnawing anxiety that keeps me awake at 3:00 AM. 

I haven't met any consultants or extra support workers. It is just the clinic. It’s just me, my supplements, and the fear that I am navigating this all by myself. Sometimes I wonder if that is how it’s meant to be here—just quiet, isolated, and polite. But a mother should not feel like she is doing this in a vacuum, should she? It feels like I am carrying the baby, and the weight of two different worlds, all on my own.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

My husband is a good man, he works so hard to make sure we have this house, this stability. But his work takes him away for days, sometimes weeks, at a time. When he is gone, the silence in this house becomes physical. It’s like a heavy blanket. 

He tries to be present on video calls, but how do you share the feeling of a baby kicking or the sharp, sudden fear when I feel faint from the iron? He can’t see the way I line up my supplements or the way I stare at the wilted cilantro in the fridge, wishing it were fresh from a market stall. To him, these appointments are just logistics—he asks, "What did the doctor say? Is the baby healthy?" and when I say "Yes, they said everything is fine," the conversation ends. He thinks he is relieving my stress by keeping things practical, but he doesn't see that I am drowning in the "why" and the "how," not just the medical facts.

When he is home, I feel like a weight has been lifted, but I’ve also learned to hide how much I’m struggling. I don't want to add to his burden when he’s already exhausted. So, I hold it in. His absence makes me the sole guardian of this pregnancy’s fears, and that is a very heavy title to hold alone.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

It is complicated. In some ways, it has made us a unit—we are focused on this baby, this future we are building here. But in the quiet moments, it feels like we are drifting on separate rafts in the same dark water. 

When he is here, I crave his touch, but I also feel a strange, sharp resentment. He gets to walk out the door, drive to the city, see other people, and interact with the world. I stay here in the snow, watching the light change, feeling every single hormonal shift and every flutter of the baby, completely alone. Sometimes, when he asks, "How are you feeling?" and I say, "I’m tired," I feel like he doesn’t understand that I don’t just mean physically tired. I mean I am tired of being an immigrant, tired of the isolation, tired of trying to be the perfect vessel for this child. 

He wants to be happy, to celebrate the "nesting," and I want to cry about the distance between us and everything we left behind. We don't fight, but we don't always reach each other, either. The pregnancy has made the glass wall I live behind feel even thicker. It is hard to be intimate when I feel like he sees a version of me that is functioning, while the real me is somewhere else, grieving the life I thought I would have as a mother.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

I wish I had a group of women here to lean on, but my social circle is... thin. My mother is a voice on the phone, thousands of miles away. When I call her, I have to be careful. I don’t want her to worry, because she can’t reach through the phone to help me, and I know that would break her heart more than it breaks mine. So, I tell her the baby is moving, that I am eating well, that I am "fine." I swallow the fear of the labor, the worry about the hospital’s distance, and the loneliness of the Wrentham winter.

Online, I have tried to look, but it is daunting. Everything feels so curated, so perfect, and so very American. I see women talking about their "birth plans" and their "aesthetic nurseries," and I feel like I am in a different universe. My worries—about whether I am nourishing the baby correctly with the limited produce I can find, or if the midwife will actually listen when I am in pain—don't seem to fit into those upbeat forums.

It feels like my internal life and my clinical life are two separate rooms, and I have no key to connect them. I turn to my own reflection in the window, to my tiny army of bottles, and to the quiet. I suppose, in the end, I am my own primary support, which is a lonely way to grow a child.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

Honestly? The only one I feel I can lean on is the grocery clerk at the small market in town. She is an older woman, not from my culture—she’s lived in these woods her whole life—but she always takes a moment to ask how the baby is doing. 

I didn't expect to share anything with a stranger in a store, but one day, I was standing in front of the produce section, just looking at a bin of sad, imported bell peppers, and I started to cry. I was missing the colors, the smell of the markets back home, and the overwhelming feeling that I was failing to feed my child properly. She didn’t look at her watch. She didn’t talk to me like I was a "medium risk" patient. She just put her hand on my arm and said, "It’s hard, isn't it? To make a home where there wasn't one before." 

That one sentence meant more to me than any of the brochures the clinic gave me. It wasn't medical, and it didn't solve my iron deficiency, but it was the first time someone in this town acknowledged that I am a person, not just a pregnancy. She is the only person here who doesn't make me feel like I am behind a glass wall. It is strange, isn't it, that the person who helps me most is just a woman selling vegetables?

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

There was this moment a few weeks ago, early in the third trimester. The midwife suggested an extra ultrasound—a growth scan, they called it—because of my age and the "medium risk" label. They mentioned it so casually, like it was just a quick stop at the post office. But for me? It was terrifying. 

I didn't have anyone to talk it through with. My husband was away, and when I messaged him, he just said, "Well, if they recommend it, you should do it to be safe." It felt like he was treating it like a car maintenance check. 

I sat there in the clinic, the midwife waiting for my "yes" or "no." I felt so pressured, not by her words, but by the weight of my own inadequacy. I wanted to ask, *Is this necessary? What if the scan shows something I can't handle? How will I cope if I’m already spiraling about my diet?* 

I didn't ask any of those things. I just nodded. I felt like if I questioned them, I’d be labeled a "difficult" patient, or worse, I’d be responsible if something went wrong. I made the decision alone, but it didn't feel like a choice. It felt like submission. I went through with the scan, and while the baby looked healthy, I left the clinic feeling hollower than before. I had performed my role as the compliant patient, but I had lost a piece of my own agency in the process. It's the price I feel I have to pay to stay "safe" in this system.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

Work has been a strange sanctuary, even if it is exhausting. I work full-time, and in the office, I am simply "Esperanza," the woman who hits her deadlines and speaks clearly. There, I am not a patient; I am a professional. But that has its own pressure. I find myself hiding my fatigue, holding my breath when the nausea hits, because I don't want them to see me as "the pregnant one." I don't want them to doubt my capabilities. It is a constant performance. I am afraid that if I show them the cracks, they will start to treat me like I am fragile, which is a different kind of imprisonment.

My cultural community feels miles and miles away. There is a small church an hour from here, but I haven't been able to go. The idea of walking into a room full of people who share my background, only to have to explain that I am struggling to find the right food or that I feel so alone in this rural silence... it feels too raw. I fear that they would see me as someone who has "lost her way" or become too Americanized. 

The people here in town—the neighbors—they are kind, but they move through life with a casual ease that I cannot replicate. They don't have to carry the weight of two languages or the memory of a different sun. Their support feels like a polite smile across a fence. It is safe, but it is not warm. I am surrounded by people, yet I am invisible to all of them. I feel like I am walking through a theater, playing a role in a play that I never auditioned for, while my real self waits in the wings, silent and waiting to be seen.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

Every day, I feel like I am being pulled apart. I look at my reflection and I see the woman I was—the one who moved across borders, the one who built a career, the one who was defined by her ambition and her voice. But now? That woman is being eclipsed by this "medium risk" label. It feels like my identity is being stripped away, layer by layer, replaced by a set of clinical charts and blood work results.

There is such a tension between being a mother and being *me*. I feel this intense pressure to be the "perfect" mother because I am so isolated. If I am not perfect—if I don't eat the right greens, if I don't follow every medical directive to the letter—then I have no one to blame but myself. I’ve lost my autonomy. I used to make decisions based on my own gut, my own intuition. Now, every decision is filtered through the fear of the "risk."

Sometimes I feel like an actress playing the role of a pregnant woman in a foreign land. I smile at the clerk, I nod at the midwife, I report the "correct" symptoms to my husband. But the real Esperanza? She is grieving. She is grieving the loss of her village, the loss of her autonomy, and the loss of the ease she thought motherhood would bring. It is a very lonely thing, to lose yourself while you are in the middle of creating life.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

It is always a cold, clinical dance. Most times, it is just me and the midwife. She is a professional, she is capable, but there is no warmth in the way she handles me. When she places the Doppler on my stomach to find the heartbeat, she doesn't tell me what she’s doing. She just moves the cold gel around, her eyes fixed on the ceiling or her notes, waiting for that *whoosh-whoosh* sound. 

I lie there, exposed and vulnerable, and I feel like an object. I want to say, "That is my son you are listening to, please be gentle," but I don't. I just stare at the cracks in the ceiling tiles and count them. The absence of my husband makes it worse. When he is not there, there is no one to hold my hand, no one to be my witness. It’s just me and the medical machine. 

There was one time a student was brought in—they didn't even ask if it was alright. They just stood there, watching. I felt like a specimen in a jar. I kept my mouth shut, but my skin felt like it was crawling. I am trying to nurture this life inside me, and they are examining the "risk" factors on my chart as if I’m not even in the room. It makes me feel small, like my body isn't my own anymore; it belongs to the clinic.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

When I had that growth scan I mentioned, I felt so incredibly exposed. The room was dark, the screen was bright, and I was lying there with my stomach cold from the gel, and all I could think was, *I am doing this all by myself.* My husband was miles away, working, and I couldn't reach out for his hand. Having no one to anchor me in that room made me feel like I was just drifting, untethered.

If he had been there, even if he didn't say anything, his presence would have changed the power dynamic. It wouldn't have been "the midwife versus me." It would have been "us" against the situation. Without him, I felt like a child being scolded by a teacher, waiting for the results of a test I didn't even understand. 

I’ve learned that in this system, silence and loneliness are often what they expect of you. It’s a very quiet, cold type of vulnerability. When you don't have an advocate by your side, you lose your voice. You stop asking questions because you don't want to be the "problem." I felt like a ghost in my own body, waiting for them to tell me if I was doing a "good job" or not. It’s not just about the medical care; it’s about having a witness to your life. Without that, you feel like you're disappearing.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

There was no one to help me navigate, really. I was left to find my own way through the labyrinth of the clinic’s portal, which is a confusing mess of digital forms and automated messages. When I moved here, I thought there would be a guide, a caseworker, or someone to explain the "rules" of the system, but there was only a list of numbers to call. 

I think the system itself made it harder. It is designed for someone who has lived here their whole life, someone who knows the rhythm of the appointments and the expectations. For me, every appointment is a guessing game. Do I ask about the supplements? Do I tell them about the fatigue? Do I mention that I’m struggling to find fresh food? Usually, I decide not to, because the atmosphere is so hurried. I don't know my rights—I only know my fears. 

I’ve had to teach myself everything by reading things online, which is just another way to fuel my anxiety. There is no human being I can call to say, "Is this normal?" or "Can you help me understand this test?" It feels like I’m in a foreign country where I’m expected to know the language of medicine, but I don’t even have a map. I am just fumbling in the dark, hoping I don't bump into something that hurts my baby. It is a very heavy, cold way to navigate the most important journey of my life.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

I wish I could say there was a friend or a mentor, but the truth is, I’ve had to be my own translator. I scour the internet at night, usually when the house is silent and the snow is reflecting the moonlight off the trees. I look up every medical term the doctor uses—*gestational hypertension, glucose screening, fetal growth percentiles*—and I try to translate the clinic’s cold, abbreviated notes into something I can understand. 

I found a few forums for mothers, but they are full of women who talk as if they were born in the waiting room. They use acronyms I’ve never heard of, and they seem to have so much confidence, questioning their doctors and demanding extra care. Reading them makes me feel like I am failing even at being a patient. 

Sometimes I try to tell my husband about what I’ve read, but he just gets worried and tells me to "stop Googling things" because it makes me anxious. He doesn't understand that for me, information is the only armor I have. If I don't know the risks, I feel even more defenseless. There is no community health worker here, no one to sit with me over a cup of coffee and say, "This is what you can expect." It’s just me, the screen, and the growing, heavy feeling that I am navigating this all wrong.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

I haven’t reached that day yet, but the thought of it hangs over me like the clouds before a storm. My husband will be there, of course, but I worry. He wants to be my protector, but in a hospital, he is just as much an outsider as I am. He will be looking at the monitors, looking for a signal from the nurses that everything is "normal." I’m afraid he will be focused on the technology, on the clinical readout, while I am drifting somewhere else, needing a touch, a word, a bridge back to myself.

I find myself wishing for my mother. I don't just want a person; I want the *energy* of someone who has walked this path before—someone who wouldn't care about the hospital’s schedule or the "medium risk" chart. Someone who would just be there to breathe with me, to remind me that my body is strong and not just a vessel for a clinical outcome.

I feel a profound, aching loneliness when I think about that room. The doctors and nurses will come and go, professional and efficient, and I know they will do their jobs. But who will be there for the *woman* in the bed? Who will witness the shift from Esperanza-the-immigrant to Esperanza-the-mother? Without a woman who understands that transition, I fear I will feel completely alone in the most crowded room of my life.
Who else was involved in the room — midwives, doctors, a doula — and how did the relationships between those people affect the atmosphere?

I haven't reached the hospital for the birth yet, but the anticipation of it feels like a heavy, cold shadow. I keep thinking about how it will be—a room full of people who don't know my name, who only know my chart. I worry that the atmosphere will be dictated by their schedules, their shift changes, and their checklists.

I have thought about a doula—someone who could be *my* person, someone to stand between me and the "system." But then I think of the cost, and the logistics, and I worry it would just be another thing to explain to my husband, another thing to feel guilty about not having. 

In my mind, I see the doctors and midwives moving with a purpose that has nothing to do with my comfort. They will talk over me, using words I don't always fully grasp, and the tension between their clinical efficiency and my need for humanity will be thick. It feels like they are the conductors of a symphony I don't know how to play. If there is no one there to advocate for me, to hold the space for my experience rather than just the medical outcome, I fear I will just go quiet. I will become small. I want to feel powerful, but I suspect I will only feel processed.
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

I haven’t had the birth yet—it feels like a dark forest waiting for me on the other side of these next few weeks. But the doctors and the clinic staff… their presence is already imprinted on me. It is a strange thing to be so watched and yet so unseen. 

The midwife I see most often? She is the one I think about the most. She isn't unkind—she remembers to check my blood pressure and asks about the baby’s movements—but her eyes are always, always moving. They scan the room, they scan the chart, they scan the clock. I feel like an interruption in her day, a brief pause in a long list of tasks. When she tells me "everything looks good," she says it to the paper, not to me. It makes me feel like I’m just a vessel being inspected for quality control.

I haven't met the labor team yet, and that, too, is a quiet terror. I imagine them coming into the room like strangers at a bus stop, shifting the rhythm of my pain to suit their hospital protocol. I wonder if any of them will look me in the eye, or if they will only look at the monitors. I am so afraid that when I am at my most vulnerable, I will be surrounded by people who are technically proficient but humanly absent. I’m just hoping for one person—just one—who will see the woman behind the "medium risk" label before I have to push my baby into this cold, quiet world.
How did that affect your trust in what was happening — did you feel like a partner in decisions or more like something was being done to you?

I feel like something is being done *to* me, rather than something I am doing. It is a slow, creeping realization. Every time I walk into the clinic, I feel my own autonomy draining away, replaced by their protocols. It’s like being a passenger in a car where the driver isn't speaking to you, but you know they have a destination you didn't choose.

I don’t feel like a partner. A partner has a voice that is heard, but my voice feels thin, easily drowned out by the authority of the white coat and the medical chart. When I ask a question, I get a brief, simplified answer that feels like it’s meant to pacify me, not inform me. It’s patronizing, even if they think they are being kind.

It makes me lose trust in my own body, too. I start to wonder if I am doing this "right," if I am truly healthy, or if I am just an accident waiting to happen because I am thirty-five and an immigrant. My trust has shifted from my own intuition to the clinic’s machines. That is a dangerous place to be. I am not a collaborator in my own care; I am a subject of it. And that feeling of helplessness… it’s the heaviest thing I’ve had to carry this whole pregnancy.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

I haven’t reached that day yet, but the weight of it is already heavy in my chest. My husband, he wants so much to be the strong one, the one who knows what to do. But I see his hands trembling when he talks about the hospital. He is terrified, too. He feels the pressure to be my protector, my buffer against the doctors and nurses, but he doesn't know the hospital's language any better than I do.

I think what he finds most challenging is the feeling of being helpless. He is a man who fixes things—if a pipe leaks, he patches it; if the car breaks, he repairs it. But he cannot fix the labor, and he cannot fix the way I feel when the nurses speak to me like I’m a child. He sees me looking at the ceiling, looking for a way out, and he doesn't know how to pull me back. 

He wants to be my anchor, but I fear he will just be another person standing there, watching the monitors and waiting for the "all clear." I don’t want him to be my advocate—I want him to be my partner. But in that clinical room, I’m afraid we’ll both be lost, two people in the middle of a process that treats us as witnesses, not participants. I want him to hold my hand, but I’m worried he’ll be staring at the screen, looking for a sign that everything is "right" instead of looking at me.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

I haven't reached that moment yet, but it’s the thing that wakes me at 3:00 AM. I imagine the monitors blaring, the room filling with people, the sudden shift in the midwife’s voice from "polite" to "urgent." 

In my mind, I see myself shrinking. When the pressure comes—the clinical, sharp kind—I fear I will lose the ability to speak. Not because I’m not smart, but because the system is designed to override me. If a doctor walks in and says, "We need to do this, now," my husband will look at me with panic, and I will look at the doctor. We will be two outsiders standing against a wave. I worry that in that moment, "my role" will simply be to stop resisting. 

I’ve seen it in the stories of other women—that feeling of being pushed along a conveyor belt, a frantic series of nods and "okay's" because you are too tired, too scared, and too intimidated to say *wait*. I am terrified that I won't be a mother making a choice for her son; I will be a body being managed by a team that thinks they know better than my own instincts. The fear isn't just the pain; it’s the erasure of my voice when it matters most.
Who else was involved — your partner, a doula, a consultant — in advocating for you or influencing what happened?

There is no one else. That is the truth of it, and it is a cold truth to admit. When I try to imagine the room, it is just me, my husband, and the people in the white coats—the ones who hold the authority, the ones who make the calls. 

I’ve thought about a doula, yes, but who would explain it to my husband? How would I justify the cost when we are trying to save for this baby’s future? And besides, I’m terrified that if I bring someone else into that room, it will only create more tension. I can’t handle more voices when I’m already struggling to hear my own intuition over the noise of the medical machines.

It feels like my husband and I are going into this battle—if you can call it that—without an interpreter, without a shield. He tries, I know he does, but he’s just as intimidated by the medical language as I am. He will look at the monitor, see the heart rate, and tell me, "The doctor knows what they’re doing, Esperanza." He doesn't realize that in those moments, I don't need someone to tell me the doctor is right; I need someone to tell me *I* am still in charge. 

The idea that I have to fight for my own voice while I am in pain… it makes me feel like I am already defeated before I even begin.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

I haven’t walked into that hospital room yet, but I can feel its weight already. I imagine a room filled with machines that beep, hum, and monitor every second of my labor. That technology is the most important "person" in the room, isn't it? The doctors and nurses gravitate toward the screen, toward the numbers, not toward my face. 

The room will be cold—that clinical, sterile cold that makes you want to curl into yourself. And the smell… that sharp, antiseptic scent that tells you *this is not home.* It will be a place of efficiency, a place where the goal is to get to the "outcome" as quickly as possible. 

My husband and I will be tiny in that space. We will be guests in a room that belongs to the hospital, not to us. When the staff comes in, the atmosphere will shift—they will bring their own pace, their own hurry, and their own language. I fear that the room will swallow us up. It will be so loud with the sounds of medical urgency that I won't be able to hear my own breath, let alone my husband’s voice. In that space, it will be so easy to feel like I’m not a woman giving birth, but a machine being serviced. I just want to find one corner of that room where I can still be *me*.
Who else was involved in shaping that environment — did anyone advocate for changes to make the space feel safer or more personal?

There is no one to ask for those things, really. When I try to imagine asking for the lights to be dimmed, or for a moment of quiet, I hear the echo of the midwife’s sigh in my head. I worry that if I ask for anything that feels "unnecessary" or "emotional," they will look at me as if I am making their job harder, as if I am being difficult when they are trying to keep us safe.

My husband, he wants me to be happy, but he is terrified of the doctors. If I suggest we ask for something—a softer touch, a different approach—he looks at me with eyes that say, "Don’t rock the boat. Just let them do their jobs so we can be finished." He sees the hospital as an authority that must be obeyed, and I see it as a place where I am slowly losing my own autonomy.

I haven’t had anyone to advocate for me, no one to tell me that it is *my* room, too. It’s just me and my own fear, negotiating for little scraps of comfort in a place that feels like it doesn't want me to feel at home. It’s as if the safety of the pregnancy is being measured only by the equipment, not by my own peace of mind. I’m just trying to make it through the door without breaking down.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

I haven’t reached my birth day yet, but the "birth plan" is a ghost that haunts my nights. I have written one down—a simple document, really. It says I want the lights low, that I want to move around, that I want the baby on my chest immediately. But when I look at that paper, I feel a strange shame. It feels like I’m writing a letter to a brick wall.

My midwife, she took the paper and tucked it into my chart without reading it while I was standing there. She smiled that polite, practiced smile and said, "We’ll see how it goes, dear." That phrase—"we’ll see"—it’s the sound of my preferences being erased. It tells me that the plan only exists as long as it’s convenient for them. 

My husband, he knows the plan, but he treats it like a suggestion rather than a map. If the doctor suggests an intervention, he’ll look at me and say, "Well, the doctor says this is safer." I know he is terrified of something going wrong, but his fear makes the plan feel fragile. I feel like I am walking toward a cliff, and the people who are supposed to be holding the safety net are already looking for ways to cut it because it doesn’t fit their hospital’s protocol. I’m scared that when I’m in the middle of it, I won’t have the strength to remind them that I am a person with wishes, not just a patient on a timeline.
How did that affect your sense of being known as a person rather than just a patient — did anyone in the room seem to really understand who you were and what mattered to you?

That is the deepest part of my loneliness. When I walk into the clinic, I am "Patient 35-Medium Risk." When I go to the hospital for the birth, I fear I will just be "The Laboring Mother in Room 4." 

No one has asked me what my favorite music is, or if I have a song that brings me peace. No one has asked about the life I left behind or why I line up my supplements like soldiers. They only care about the things that can be measured—the blood pressure, the heart rate, the cervix. If these things are "normal," they act as if I am perfectly fine, even when I am trembling on the inside. 

I feel like a ghost in the machine. I want to say, "I am a woman who loves the sun, who is terrified of the snow, and who misses the taste of home," but there is no space for that in a fifteen-minute appointment. I am just a series of data points on a screen. The only person who sees *me*—really sees the fear and the effort—is the woman at the market who sells me the vegetables. And she won’t be in the delivery room. 

I am so afraid that when I am at my most vulnerable, I will be completely invisible. It is a terrible thing to be surrounded by people who are technically "caring" for you, yet none of them knows the woman they are helping bring a life into the world.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I first thought about this pregnancy, I felt hopeful, naive even. Back in my home country, there was an older midwife in my neighborhood. I didn't see her for my medical care, but she was the person everyone went to. She knew our names, she knew our mothers, and she knew the way we carried our stresses. When she spoke to you, she didn't look at a chart; she looked into your eyes. You could trust her because she was a witness to your life, not just a spectator to your health.

Here? I haven’t found anyone like that. My GP is a man who speaks in short, hurried sentences about "risk factors" and "age-related concerns." He treats my body like a machine that is starting to show its wear and tear. I want to tell him about how I feel, but his air of detached authority makes me feel like I’m wasting his time if I mention anything other than a physical symptom.

I suppose that is what I’m mourning—the loss of the *personal* in the professional. I don’t want to be "managed." I want to be known. I thought that by coming here, I would find the best care in the world, but I’ve found that "best" just means efficient and cold. It’s hard to trust someone who doesn't see you. I’m just a chart in a digital folder, and that is a very lonely, hollow way to grow a life.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

It was during my second trimester when I brought up the sheer intensity of the isolation I felt. I sat there, trying to explain that the rural quiet wasn't just peaceful—it was eroding my sense of self, making me doubt my ability to be a mother in a culture that felt so foreign. I wanted someone to say, "I hear you, and that is a normal, difficult part of your journey."

Instead, the midwife barely looked up from her computer screen. She clicked a few buttons and said, "Your blood pressure is stable, and the baby is growing on schedule. You’re doing great, Esperanza." 

That was it. She didn't hear the "I'm scared" or the "I'm lonely." She only heard the "stable" and "on schedule." In that moment, my trust didn't just shake; it broke. It made me realize that my emotional health—my *humanity*—was irrelevant to them as long as the medical measurements stayed within the lines. 

I felt so small. I wanted to scream that a healthy baby isn't the only thing that matters, that the mother’s heart matters too. But I stayed silent. I just nodded, accepted the next appointment time, and walked out to my car. I sat in the parking lot and cried because I realized then that I am entirely on my own in this system. I am a patient to be monitored, not a woman to be cared for.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Looking back, the tone was always so clinical, so dismissive. When I first discussed my health, my age—being thirty-five—was immediately placed at the center of the room. It wasn’t a conversation about my life or my goals; it was a conversation about my "biological clock." 

The doctor spoke as if he were reading from a textbook on high-risk factors. He didn’t ask what I wanted or how I felt about my body. He just laid out the risks like they were warning signs on a dangerous road. I remember feeling like I was being told what to do with my own life by a stranger who didn't know anything about who I was or what I valued. 

It was always "You should do this," or "We need to monitor that," as if my input was secondary to the medical checklist. I felt like a child being guided through a system that had already decided what my path should be. I wanted to be a partner in that, to ask questions that were meaningful to me, but the authority was always tipped toward them. It’s hard to feel like you have a voice when you’re constantly being told that your age is the only thing that matters. That was when I first realized that being a patient here meant relinquishing my own agency.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

It was just me and the OB-GYN in that small, sterile room. My husband wasn't there—he couldn't take the time off for what he called a "routine chat." I remember the way the doctor leaned back in his chair, his hands clasped over a manila folder that held my entire history, as if my whole life could be summarized by the dates and test results inside.

He didn't ask about my life in Wrentham or how I felt about moving here. He just started listing numbers. "At thirty-five," he said, and the way he said it made my skin prickle, "we consider this advanced maternal age. We need to be vigilant." The word 'vigilant' felt like a threat. It felt like he was telling me my body was already failing, that I was a liability.

I felt so exposed, sitting on that crinkly paper that sounded like a warning every time I shifted. I wanted to say, "But I feel strong," or "My mother had children at my age," but I knew he’d just dismiss it as anecdotal. I felt like a student being told I was going to fail a test I hadn't even taken yet. It was the moment I realized that in this system, my role was to be a silent recipient of their expertise. I left that room feeling like I was walking on eggshells in my own skin.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before I became pregnant, I viewed my body as a bridge—the vessel that carried me from the life I knew in my country to the one I was building here. It was strong. It was resilient. I was proud of it, honestly. I worked, I traveled, I felt capable. Pregnancy was always something I envisioned as a natural, almost rhythmic transition, the way it was for the women in my family.

I didn’t talk about it openly, though. I think that was my first mistake. In my culture, we speak of these things with a sort of reverence, a quiet shared knowledge between sisters and mothers. But here, that intimacy doesn't exist. When I mentioned to a colleague that I was thinking about starting a family, she immediately started talking about "fertility windows" and "career impacts." It made the whole idea of bringing life into the world sound like a business strategy, something to be optimized and scheduled.

It felt like the joy was being drained out of it, replaced by this clinical anxiety. I stopped talking about it because I didn't want to hear about the "risks" or the "timelines." I wanted to hear about the feeling of a baby kicking, or the way your life expands to hold another person. But here, the only language for pregnancy is the language of risk. And slowly, that began to change how I felt about my body—it went from being a strong, capable home for my soul to a delicate, "medium risk" machine that I constantly worry will break.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

It was exhausting. I started with the big, glossy apps—the ones everyone uses here. But quickly, I felt like they were written by someone who had never actually sat in a doctor’s office feeling small. They’d say things like, "Ask your doctor anything!" or "You are in control!" and I’d just laugh, a bitter, quiet sound. My reality was so far from those chirpy, cheerful push notifications.

I found myself gravitating toward forums where women—real women, not medical experts—talked about their fears. But even that was a trap. One minute I’d read a comment about how "natural" birth is, and the next, a story about a medical complication that would keep me awake until dawn. I had to stop. I realized that the more I "informed" myself through those screens, the more my anxiety spiked. I was looking for certainty in a system that only offers probabilities.

I wanted a source that felt like *me*—someone who understood the cultural weight of motherhood, the isolation of being an immigrant in these woods, and the reality of the clinic’s indifference. I never found it. I ended up trusting my own gut less and the clinic’s charts more, which I know is the opposite of what I should have done. I was just drowning in information, and none of it made me feel like I was holding the steering wheel.
Did you ever come across advice or information that felt hard to access — perhaps because of cost, language, or just not knowing where to start?

It wasn’t just the information that was hard to access—it was the *context* behind it. When I’d read about "nutritional support" or "mental health resources," everything felt designed for a woman who could just pop into a local center or afford a private therapist. I’m in rural Wrentham. There is no center. There is only the clinic, thirty minutes away, and my kitchen counter covered in supplement bottles.

I wanted to find someone who could tell me which local greens were actually healthy substitutes for the ones I grew up with—practical, cultural advice—but all I found were generic, impersonal food pyramids. It felt like I was being handed a map to a city I didn't live in. 

And the language… it isn't that I don't understand the English words; I do. It’s that the *meaning* of the words is different. When they say "you are low risk" or "don't worry," to them it’s a standard reassurance. To me, it feels like a dismissal of the complex, heavy reality of being a woman alone in a foreign landscape. I didn't need more pamphlets on "healthy habits." I needed a human who could bridge the gap between my culture and this sterile, snowy world. Everything felt locked behind a door I didn't have the key to open.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

Before I even saw a positive test, I felt like I was already carrying a scarlet letter. Being thirty-five, an "advanced maternal age," was the first thing they threw at me. It felt like I was starting a race already five miles behind. I would walk into the clinic feeling healthy—strong from my walks, vibrant from my work—and walk out feeling like I was an accident waiting to happen, a "geriatric" pregnancy in the making. 

I’m Hispanic, and that brought its own silent pressures. I noticed the way they’d look at my diet, the way they’d suggest I change things I had eaten my entire life for something more "American" and "standardized." It felt like they were trying to bleach the culture out of my body to make me "safer." 

It made me deeply defensive, but also incredibly ashamed. I stopped trusting my own body’s instincts because I was so convinced that the doctor saw a ticking time bomb instead of a woman. It’s a terrible feeling, knowing that before you’ve even had a chance to bloom, someone has already categorized you as a failure, a risk, a problem to be solved. That feeling of being "wrong" or "inadequate" became my constant shadow, even before the baby was even a reality.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

It happened so many times I stopped counting, but one day sticks with me. I was feeling particularly drained—the kind of fatigue that sits in your bones—and I mentioned it to the midwife. I also brought up how much I missed the fresh, vibrant foods of my culture, and how hard it was to find anything that felt "real" in the supermarket here.

She didn't look at me. She just typed something into the computer, sighed, and said, "We’re really concerned about your iron levels, Esperanza. You need to focus on the supplements we prescribed and stick to the dietary guidelines we gave you. The fatigue is likely tied to your age and your hemoglobin."

I felt my heart drop. She hadn't heard a single word about my loneliness or my cultural hunger. She had just taken my feelings and turned them into a "condition" that needed "fixing." It felt as though my identity—my background, my feelings, my struggle with being an immigrant in these woods—was just white noise that kept her from the "real" medical work. I wasn't Esperanza; I was a collection of deficiencies. I felt like I had to apologize for being human. It made me realize that to be "seen" by them, I have to be quiet, compliant, and completely stripped of everything that makes me *me*.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

Before we decided to try, we talked, of course. My husband was eager, excited about building a family in this house we worked so hard to buy. But the "how" and the "what if" were always discussions between us, in the safety of our own home. When we finally brought the medical side into it—the preconception check-ups—he was present, but his role was almost entirely passive. 

He’d sit in the chair next to me in the clinic, mostly silent, letting me field the questions about my cycles and my health. He seemed to view the doctor as the absolute authority, someone you didn't question. If the doctor said "you should do this," my husband would turn to me with a reassuring look that said, "See? They know what's best." 

He wasn't trying to be dismissive, but he didn't realize that I was looking for a partner who would ask *why* or *is there another way?* Instead, I felt like I was being herded into a specific, clinical version of pregnancy before I’d even conceived. His presence was a comfort, but it was also a shield for the doctor’s agenda. He saw the doctor as a guide, while I saw a gatekeeper who was already closing doors on my own intuition. It made me feel very much like I was navigating a system that he was perfectly happy to just follow.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

When he comes, the dynamic shifts instantly. The midwife suddenly speaks to *both* of us, but often her gaze lingers on him as if he’s the one who needs the reassurance, or as if he’s the one who can actually make the "important" decisions. It’s subtle, but I feel myself shrink. It’s like I’m no longer the one being examined; I’m just the medium through which the medical information passes, and he’s the one expected to process it.

He tries to be helpful, asking, "Is she okay? Is everything on track?"—but he’s asking for a snapshot, a guarantee. When the midwife gives him that, he relaxes, satisfied. But his presence means I can’t be honest about my inner life. I don’t want to worry him, so I put on the mask of the compliant, steady partner. I hold my breath and don't mention the tears I cried in the car or the panic I feel about the birth. 

He acts like a buffer, but he’s really a wall. His presence makes the room feel safer for *him*, but it makes it harder for me to be vulnerable. The midwife talks to the "couple," but she still doesn't see the woman. I end up feeling even more isolated, standing right next to the person I love most, yet unable to bridge the gap between our two realities.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

There was one moment, long before I was pregnant, when I had a primary care doctor who actually listened. I had come in with a persistent, nagging pain, and she didn't rush. She pulled up a chair and said, "Tell me what’s happening in your life, not just in your body." I remember feeling a weight lift. Because she asked, I felt safe enough to tell her about the stress of my job and how I was feeling about our plan to start a family. I felt like a human being, not a case study. In that moment, my voice felt like it had power, like it was the most important diagnostic tool in the room.

But those moments are so rare they feel like ghosts. Mostly, my voice disappears the second I step through those clinic doors. When I’ve tried to suggest something different—like wanting to space out tests or asking for an alternative to a specific medication—the air in the room changes. The smile stays, but the eyes turn cold, and the explanation becomes patronizing, as if I’m a child who just doesn't understand "the science." 

It’s that feeling—of being dismissed with a polite, practiced nod—that hurts the most. It tells me that my own intuition, the thing that has guided me through moving across an ocean and building a life in a new land, means nothing compared to their standard protocols. My voice isn't heard; it’s filtered, ignored, or silenced. It’s like being a character in a book where someone else holds the pen and can delete my words whenever they become inconvenient.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

At the beginning, I think I was waiting for someone to give me the map. I trusted that if I followed the instructions—the supplements, the tests, the guidelines—I would be "safe." But the further I get, the more I realize that the clinic isn't interested in my safety as a whole person; they are only interested in a sterile, clinical outcome. That realization has changed everything for me.

Now, my deepest wish is to reclaim some sense of ownership. I’ve stopped looking for permission to feel or to ask questions. I’ve started, very quietly, to keep my own notes—not about my blood pressure, but about how I feel, what I fear, and what I *actually* want for the birth. I don't share these with the midwife anymore, because I know she’ll just dismiss them. Instead, I’m building a small, internal fortress. 

I’ve realized that I don't want a "manager" of my pregnancy; I want an ally. Since I haven't found one in the clinic, I am becoming my own advocate, even if it feels terrifying. It’s a lonely shift, moving from a compliant patient to someone who is silently resisting, but it’s the only way I can protect my dignity. I don't want to be "processed" through labor—I want to be present in it. If I can’t change how they see me, I can at least change how I see myself.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

I truly wish I could say there was a moment in that clinic where I felt like the expert of my own body, but there hasn't been one. Not once. When I try to speak about how I feel—the specific kind of bone-deep fatigue or the way my body reacts to the stress of being an immigrant in these woods—the response is always a clinical redirection. It is as if they believe that unless they see it on a monitor or a lab report, it isn't real.

I remember once trying to explain that the prenatal vitamin they gave me was making me violently ill—the metallic taste, the nausea that left me shaking. I didn't want to just stop taking it, I wanted to find a solution *with* them. Instead of listening, the midwife just leaned back, tapped her pen on her clipboard, and said, "It’s standard, Esperanza. The nausea is just part of the pregnancy. You have to push through it for the baby's sake."

She made me feel like my discomfort was a lack of willpower, not a valid physical response. In that room, my intuition—the voice that tells me when something is wrong—is treated like an inconvenient noise. I am the one carrying this child, I am the one living in this body twenty-four hours a day, yet I am the person whose input is valued the least. It’s a very cold feeling, being a stranger to your own healthcare provider.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

My husband tries to be involved, but he is involved in the way someone is involved in a building project: he wants the project to finish, and he wants it to be "to code." When we look at the birth plan or discuss an upcoming appointment, his questions are always about the medical checklist. "Did you ask the doctor about X? What did they say about the risk level?" 

Sometimes I feel like he is an extension of the medical system rather than an extension of me. He believes the doctor's authority is absolute. When I try to share my doubts, he feels anxious, like I am bringing chaos into a process that he wants to be controlled and predictable. He tells me to "trust the experts," and in doing so, he unintentionally strips me of my own voice.

I feel a strange, lonely resentment when he acts this way. I want him to be the one who asks, "How is this treatment making Esperanza feel?" or "Does she have any questions for you?" instead of just nodding at the midwife. I want him to witness my struggle, not just manage the outcome of the pregnancy. His involvement, while well-intentioned, often leaves me feeling even more isolated, as if I have two doctors in the room and neither one of them is truly hearing the woman inside the patient's chair.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

When my husband is in the room, the midwife’s demeanor changes. She stops looking at me entirely. She directs her explanations to him, using shorter sentences and a tone that is somehow more patronizing than usual. It’s as if she thinks he’s the "decision-maker" and I’m just the... the vessel. He sits there nodding, and I feel myself getting smaller and smaller in that chair.

I stop myself from asking the hard questions when he’s there. I don’t want to look like the "difficult" one in front of him, and I don't want to cause him any more stress because he’s already so anxious about the "risk." So I stay silent. I watch them talk about me in the third person, about my blood pressure and my iron levels, while I just stare at the wall. 

It feels like my presence is a formality. If he weren't there, I’d be a patient; when he *is* there, I’m a patient’s spouse. Neither role lets me be the person who is actually carrying this baby. His presence turns a medical appointment into a performance where I have to play the part of the calm, happy, obedient expectant mother, even while my insides are churning with everything I’m holding back. It’s exhausting.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

My relationship with my body has become a battleground. Before, I was the one who controlled it—I moved, I worked, I traveled. Now, it feels like it belongs to the clinic. Every time they hook me up to a monitor or hand me a bottle of pills to guard against "risk," I feel a little less like a person and more like a vessel. 

There are days when I feel a profound, fierce connection to the baby—the way he kicks, the way I know his rhythm when it’s just the two of us. But then I walk into that clinic, and the cold gel, the silence, and the dismissiveness of the midwife make me feel like a failing machine. It’s hard to sit with that. I feel like my body is being judged for not being "textbook" enough, for being thirty-five, for being tired.

I haven't found anyone I can truly talk to about this—not really. Not in a way that doesn't sound like a complaint or an admission of weakness. When I talk to my husband, he just wants to solve it, to give me "solutions." When I talk to my mother, I lie because I don't want to break her heart. I sit in my car in the parking lot and cry, then wipe my face before I go back to work. I’m carrying this heavy, shifting feeling of disconnect entirely alone.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

The comment that stung the most came from the midwife during one of my routine check-ups. I was feeling particularly sluggish, and I made the mistake of saying, "I feel like I'm losing myself, like my body isn't my own anymore." 

She didn't pause. She didn't look up. She just tapped her pen on the desk and said, "That’s just the price of being a mother, Esperanza. You’re doing it for the baby, aren't you?"

It was meant to be a comfort, I suppose—a way to "reframing" my struggle as a noble sacrifice. But it felt like a door being slammed shut. It told me that my identity, my autonomy, and my discomfort were all things that should be silenced, all things I should be willing to trade away without complaint. It made me feel like my body was no longer a person; it was just a piece of property, a tool to be used until the "result" was delivered.

My husband, too, unintentionally makes it worse. When he touches my stomach, he talks about the baby as if he's checking on a package. He doesn't ask about *my* fatigue or the way my skin feels tight and strange. It's like I’m a glowing, holy vessel for his son, but the woman inside that vessel is being ignored. I am treated as a site of production, not a person in transition. It’s a very lonely kind of objectification.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I suppose... I just want to say that being "safe" and being "cared for" are not the same thing. Everyone in the clinic is focused on safety—on the numbers, the risk, the healthy baby at the end of the line. But while they are securing my safety, they are letting my spirit wither away. 

I’ve spent these months feeling like I’m in a cold, white room, shouting into a glass wall. I am not asking for the moon; I am asking for someone to look at me, to hear the tremor in my voice when I talk about the loneliness, and to treat me like a person who has crossed oceans, not just a chart in a folder. 

I am thirty-five. I am a mother. I am an immigrant. I am a professional. I am all of these things, yet in the clinic, I am reduced to a "medium risk." I wish someone had just asked, *“Esperanza, what do you need to feel strong today?”* instead of *“Esperanza, did you take your iron?”* 

If you can tell them anything, tell them that we are not just cases to be closed. We are human beings carrying the future, and we need to be held as much as we need to be monitored. That’s all. Just to be known.
